title: Polymer clay miniature succulent
subtitle: A sculpted succulent plant in a small clay pot, baked and permanent
excerpt: A polymer clay miniature succulent is built leaf by leaf from individually shaped and assembled clay petals, then baked hard in a home oven. The finished plant is permanent and needs no watering. Building a realistic rosette from 12 to 20 petals takes patience but no specialist tools. No kiln is needed.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay botanical sculpture technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Polymer clay
is the right material for miniature botanical forms because it does not shrink or crack during
curing

, and thin petal sections survive the bake without breaking. A succulent rosette is built from the outside inward: the largest outer leaves first, the tight central bud last. Each layer is assembled on a ball of clay that becomes the plant's base.

attrs.body: Echeveria-style succulents range from grey-green to pink-purple at the leaf tips. Mix a base green from yellow and blue clay, then add small amounts of purple or pink to a separate batch for the leaf tips. Apply the tip colour by pressing it onto the leaf edges before shaping.
attrs.title: Colour mixing

content.text: What you need

items.name: Polymer clay, green (and pink or purple for tips)
Small clay pot or bottle cap (base for the plant)

content.text: Building the succulent
Step 1: Make the base
Roll a ball of green clay 2.5 cm in diameter. This becomes the core that the leaves attach to. Flatten the bottom so it sits in the pot without rolling. Do not bake it yet.
Step 2: Shape the outer leaves

Pinch a pea-sized piece of green clay into a teardrop shape, then flatten it slightly. The leaf should be 2 to 3 cm long, thicker at the base and tapering to a point at the tip. Press the tip slightly upward by curling the leaf away from the flat face. Make eight to ten outer leaves in the same way.
Step 3: Attach the outer ring

Press the flat base of each outer leaf against the clay ball near the bottom, spreading them outward around the circumference. The leaves should overlap slightly and angle downward and outward. Press each leaf firmly against the ball so the join is secure.
Step 4: Build inner rings and bud

Make progressively smaller leaves for each inner ring, angling them more upright as they get closer to the centre. The innermost 'leaves' are tiny tightly-curled scrolls about 5 mm long. Apply them standing almost vertical around the central point. The centremost piece is a tiny cone or tight scroll. Use the needle tool to press each piece into position and blend the base against the core.
Step 5: Bake and pot

Set the assembled succulent in a bottle cap or small clay pot to keep it upright during baking. Bake at 130°C for 30 minutes. Cool completely. Press the base of the plant into a small pot with a bed of raw clay inside, then bake the whole assembly for a further 20 minutes to secure the plant in its pot.
Variations
Make three different-sized succulents and arrange them in a shallow tray with polymer clay pebbles.
Add veining to each leaf by pressing with the needle tool tip before assembling.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Problems when building the miniature succulent.

items.fix: Press the leaf base firmly for a count of three. If the clay is too warm and sticky, refrigerate the core for 10 minutes before continuing.
items.cause: Leaf base not pressed firmly enough against the core, or clay too warm and tacky.
items.symptom: Leaves fall off the core during assembly

items.fix: The outer ring should use the largest leaves and the inner rings should be noticeably smaller. Plan three or four size grades before starting.
items.cause: Not decreasing leaf size toward the centre.
items.symptom: Leaves all look the same size

attrs.heading: Common problems
